questions.prompt: Which research method establishes cause-and-effect by manipulating an independent variable while controlling for confounds?
questions.correctText: Experiment
questions.topic: Unit 0: Science of Psychology — Research Methods

questions.prompt: A researcher randomly assigns participants to two groups. This procedure primarily helps to:
choices: Eliminate the need for a control group
questions.correctText: Reduce confounding variables between groups

questions.prompt: A correlation coefficient of -0.85 indicates:
questions.correctText: A strong negative relationship

questions.prompt: The DSM-5 is primarily used to:
questions.correctText: Diagnose psychological disorders

questions.prompt: Which ethical principle requires that participants be told the true nature of a study after it ends?
questions.correctText: Debriefing

questions.prompt: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with the brain's reward pathway and is implicated in addiction?
questions.correctText: Dopamine

questions.prompt: Damage to which brain structure most directly impairs the formation of new long-term memories?
questions.correctText: Hippocampus

questions.prompt: The fight-or-flight response is primarily controlled by which branch of the autonomic nervous system?
questions.correctText: Sympathetic

questions.prompt: Phineas Gage's personality change after his accident provided early evidence for the function of which brain region?
questions.correctText: Frontal lobe

questions.prompt: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with mood regulation and is the target of SSRI antidepressants?
questions.correctText: Serotonin

questions.prompt: The all-or-none principle states that a neuron will:
choices: Fire at varying strengths depending on input
Fire only when threshold is crossed, always at the same intensity
Fire only in response to inhibitory signals
Never fire if any inhibitory input is present
questions.correctText: Fire only when threshold is crossed, always at the same intensity

questions.prompt: Which lobe of the brain is most responsible for processing visual information?
questions.correctText: Occipital

questions.prompt: Broca's area, located in the frontal lobe, is primarily involved in:
questions.correctText: Speech production

questions.prompt: Identical twins raised apart who show similar IQ scores provide evidence for which influence on intelligence?
questions.correctText: Heredity

questions.prompt: The endocrine system communicates via:
choices: Reflex arcs in the spinal cord
questions.correctText: Hormones released into the bloodstream

questions.prompt: REM sleep is characterized by:
choices: Deep relaxation and slow brain waves
Vivid dreaming and rapid eye movement
The first stage of falling asleep
questions.correctText: Vivid dreaming and rapid eye movement

questions.prompt: Which psychologist's experiments with dogs and bells established classical conditioning?
questions.correctText: Ivan Pavlov

questions.prompt: In Pavlov's experiment, the meat powder is the:
questions.correctText: Unconditioned stimulus

questions.prompt: A child stops touching a hot stove after burning themselves once. This is an example of:
questions.correctText: Operant conditioning via positive punishment

questions.prompt: Reinforcement schedules that produce the highest, most consistent rate of responding are:
questions.correctText: Variable ratio

questions.prompt: Albert Bandura's Bobo doll experiment demonstrated:
questions.correctText: Observational learning of aggression

questions.prompt: Information held in short-term memory typically lasts how long without rehearsal?
questions.correctText: About 20-30 seconds

questions.prompt: George Miller's research showed that short-term memory capacity is approximately:
questions.correctText: 7 ± 2 items

questions.prompt: Memorizing a phone number by grouping digits is an example of:
questions.correctText: Chunking

questions.prompt: The misinformation effect, demonstrated by Elizabeth Loftus, shows that:
choices: Eyewitness memory can be altered by leading questions
Memory is perfectly stored like a recording
questions.correctText: Eyewitness memory can be altered by leading questions

questions.prompt: Procedural memory is best illustrated by:
questions.correctText: Riding a bicycle

questions.prompt: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is an example of:
questions.correctText: Retrieval failure

questions.prompt: Which thinking error involves searching only for information that confirms what you already believe?
questions.correctText: Confirmation bias

questions.prompt: Estimating the frequency of an event based on how easily examples come to mind is the:
questions.correctText: Availability heuristic

questions.prompt: Solving a problem suddenly without conscious step-by-step reasoning is called:
questions.correctText: Insight learning

questions.prompt: Linguistic relativity (the Whorfian hypothesis) suggests that:
choices: All humans share identical language structures
Language is innate and learned without exposure
The language we speak influences how we think
Animals can be taught full human language
questions.correctText: The language we speak influences how we think

questions.prompt: Piaget's stage in which children develop object permanence is:
questions.correctText: Sensorimotor

questions.prompt: Erikson identified the primary developmental task of adolescence as:
questions.correctText: Identity vs. role confusion

questions.prompt: Harry Harlow's monkey experiments demonstrated that infants attach most strongly to caregivers who provide:
questions.correctText: Comfort and contact

questions.prompt: An authoritative parenting style is characterized by:
questions.correctText: High demands AND high responsiveness

questions.prompt: Kohlberg's preconventional level of moral reasoning is primarily based on:
questions.correctText: Avoiding punishment and gaining rewards

questions.prompt: Which sensory threshold is the smallest difference between two stimuli that can be detected 50% of the time?
questions.correctText: Just-noticeable difference (Weber's law)

questions.prompt: Color blindness most commonly results from a problem with:
questions.correctText: Cones in the retina

questions.prompt: The phenomenon by which familiar objects appear the same size regardless of viewing distance is called:
questions.correctText: Size constancy

questions.prompt: Gestalt psychologists emphasized that:
choices: Behavior can only be measured through reflexes
The whole is greater than the sum of the parts
Mental processes can never be studied scientifically
questions.correctText: The whole is greater than the sum of the parts

questions.prompt: Top-down processing relies primarily on:
questions.correctText: Past experience and expectations

questions.prompt: The McGurk effect demonstrates that:
choices: Vision and hearing are processed independently
Visual cues influence what we hear
questions.correctText: Visual cues influence what we hear

questions.prompt: Spearman's 'g' refers to:
choices: General intelligence underlying all mental abilities
questions.correctText: General intelligence underlying all mental abilities

questions.prompt: An IQ test's reliability refers to:
choices: Whether it measures what it claims to measure
How consistent its scores are across administrations
Whether the test is fair across cultures
How quickly the test can be administered
questions.correctText: How consistent its scores are across administrations

questions.prompt: Maslow's hierarchy of needs places which need at the top?
questions.correctText: Self-actualization

questions.prompt: Drive-reduction theory explains motivation as the body's attempt to:
questions.correctText: Restore physiological homeostasis

questions.prompt: The James-Lange theory of emotion proposes that:
choices: We feel emotion first, then the body responds
Body response and emotion happen simultaneously
We notice the body's response and then experience the emotion
questions.correctText: We notice the body's response and then experience the emotion

questions.prompt: The two-factor theory of emotion (Schachter-Singer) requires:
choices: Both physiological arousal AND cognitive labeling
questions.correctText: Both physiological arousal AND cognitive labeling

questions.prompt: Selye's general adaptation syndrome stages are:
questions.correctText: Alarm, resistance, exhaustion

questions.prompt: Freud's defense mechanism in which unacceptable impulses are pushed out of conscious awareness is called:
questions.correctText: Repression

questions.prompt: Carl Rogers's person-centered therapy emphasizes:
questions.correctText: Unconditional positive regard

questions.prompt: The Big Five personality model includes which trait?
questions.correctText: Conscientiousness

questions.prompt: An individual with a strong internal locus of control most likely believes that:
choices: Luck and fate determine their outcomes
Their own effort determines their outcomes
questions.correctText: Their own effort determines their outcomes

questions.prompt: Bandura's concept of self-efficacy refers to:
choices: One's belief in one's own ability to succeed
The unconscious mind's influence on behavior
questions.correctText: One's belief in one's own ability to succeed

questions.prompt: Hallucinations and delusions are positive symptoms most associated with:
questions.correctText: Schizophrenia

questions.prompt: Persistent, intrusive thoughts paired with repetitive behaviors aimed at reducing anxiety characterize:
questions.correctText: Obsessive-compulsive disorder

questions.prompt: Bipolar disorder is characterized by:
choices: Alternating episodes of mania and depression
questions.correctText: Alternating episodes of mania and depression

questions.prompt: A panic attack is most associated with which class of disorder?
questions.correctText: Anxiety

questions.prompt: Cognitive therapy for depression (Aaron Beck) primarily focuses on:
questions.correctText: Maladaptive thoughts and cognitive distortions

questions.prompt: Systematic desensitization is a behavior therapy primarily used to treat:
questions.correctText: Phobias

questions.prompt: Antipsychotic medications primarily target which neurotransmitter system?
questions.correctText: Dopamine

questions.prompt: SSRIs increase the availability of:
questions.correctText: Serotonin

questions.prompt: Solomon Asch's conformity studies showed that participants conformed to incorrect group answers about:
choices: 75% of the time on every trial
Roughly one third of trials on average
Never, when the answer was obvious
Only when the group was very small
questions.correctText: Roughly one third of trials on average

questions.prompt: Stanley Milgram's obedience study found that approximately what percentage of participants delivered the maximum shock?
questions.correctText: Roughly 65%

questions.prompt: The bystander effect is best explained by:
questions.correctText: Diffusion of responsibility

questions.prompt: Cognitive dissonance theory predicts that people change their attitudes to:
choices: Avoid all forms of mental effort
questions.correctText: Reduce inconsistency with their behavior

questions.prompt: The fundamental attribution error involves:
choices: Overestimating situational factors in others' behavior
Overestimating dispositional (personality) factors in others' behavior
Always blaming oneself for negative events
Refusing to make any judgments about behavior
questions.correctText: Overestimating dispositional (personality) factors in others' behavior

questions.prompt: Stereotype threat refers to:
choices: The threat of being attacked due to one's identity
Underperformance caused by awareness of a negative stereotype about one's group
An increase in performance when stereotyped positively
Loss of cultural identity through assimilation
questions.correctText: Underperformance caused by awareness of a negative stereotype about one's group

questions.prompt: In-group bias is the tendency to:
choices: Favor members of one's own group over outsiders
questions.correctText: Favor members of one's own group over outsiders

questions.prompt: Zimbardo's Stanford Prison Experiment is often cited as evidence for:
choices: The power of social roles and situations to shape behavior
questions.correctText: The power of social roles and situations to shape behavior

questions.prompt: Robert Cialdini's principles of social influence include:
questions.correctText: Reciprocity and scarcity

questions.prompt: Group polarization typically results in:
choices: The majority adopting the minority view
Group decisions that are more extreme than initial individual opinions
questions.correctText: Group decisions that are more extreme than initial individual opinions

questions.prompt: Groupthink is most likely when:
choices: Group members have diverse opinions and challenge each other openly
A group is highly cohesive and isolated from outside perspectives
A group includes outside consultants and devil's advocates
Members are anonymous and don't know each other
questions.correctText: A group is highly cohesive and isolated from outside perspectives

questions.prompt: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with muscle contraction at the neuromuscular junction?
questions.correctText: Acetylcholine

questions.prompt: GABA functions primarily as which type of neurotransmitter in the central nervous system?
choices: A hormone released by the pituitary
A modulator of the autonomic nervous system only
questions.correctText: The brain's main inhibitory neurotransmitter

questions.prompt: Which neurotransmitter is the brain's primary excitatory neurotransmitter, critical to learning and memory?
questions.correctText: Glutamate

questions.prompt: Endorphins are most directly involved in:
choices: Reducing pain and producing pleasurable feelings
questions.correctText: Reducing pain and producing pleasurable feelings

questions.prompt: Norepinephrine is most directly associated with:
questions.correctText: Arousal and alertness

questions.prompt: Low levels of acetylcholine in the brain have been linked to which disorder?
questions.correctText: Alzheimer's disease

questions.prompt: Parkinson's disease is most directly linked to a deficiency of which neurotransmitter?
questions.correctText: Dopamine

questions.prompt: Agonist drugs work by:
choices: Mimicking or enhancing the effects of a neurotransmitter
questions.correctText: Mimicking or enhancing the effects of a neurotransmitter

questions.prompt: Antagonist drugs work by:
choices: Increasing neurotransmitter release into the synapse
questions.correctText: Blocking neurotransmitter activity at receptors

questions.prompt: The space between two neurons where neurotransmitters are released is called the:
questions.correctText: Synapse

questions.prompt: The myelin sheath's primary function is to:
choices: Maintain ion balance during action potentials
questions.correctText: Speed up neural transmission

questions.prompt: Depolarization of a neuron's membrane refers to:
choices: The neuron returning to its resting potential
The membrane potential moving toward zero or positive
questions.correctText: The membrane potential moving toward zero or positive

questions.prompt: The refractory period is best defined as:
choices: The brief time after firing when a neuron cannot fire again
The period before the action potential reaches threshold
questions.correctText: The brief time after firing when a neuron cannot fire again

questions.prompt: Resting potential of a typical neuron is approximately:
questions.correctText: -70 mV

questions.prompt: Excitatory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs) increase the likelihood that a neuron will:
choices: Reach threshold and fire an action potential
Hyperpolarize and become less likely to fire
questions.correctText: Reach threshold and fire an action potential

questions.prompt: The peripheral nervous system is composed of the somatic and ____ nervous systems.
questions.correctText: Autonomic

questions.prompt: The parasympathetic nervous system is primarily responsible for:
choices: Calming the body and conserving energy
Conducting reflex arcs in the spinal cord
questions.correctText: Calming the body and conserving energy

questions.prompt: The somatic nervous system controls:
questions.correctText: Voluntary skeletal muscle movement

questions.prompt: A simple reflex arc, such as withdrawing your hand from a hot stove, is mediated primarily by the:
questions.correctText: Spinal cord

questions.prompt: Which structure relays sensory information (except smell) to the appropriate cortical areas?
questions.correctText: Thalamus

questions.prompt: Damage to the cerebellum would most likely impair:
questions.correctText: Balance and coordinated movement

questions.prompt: The medulla is critical for:
questions.correctText: Heartbeat and breathing

questions.prompt: The reticular formation is most associated with:
questions.correctText: Arousal and consciousness

questions.prompt: The pons is most directly involved in:
questions.correctText: Sleep and arousal

questions.prompt: The amygdala is most associated with processing which emotion?
questions.correctText: Fear

questions.prompt: Damage to the hypothalamus would most likely disrupt:
choices: Hunger, thirst, and body temperature regulation
questions.correctText: Hunger, thirst, and body temperature regulation

questions.prompt: Patient H.M., who had his hippocampus removed, lost the ability to:
questions.correctText: Form new long-term memories

questions.prompt: Wernicke's area is primarily involved in:
questions.correctText: Language comprehension

questions.prompt: The motor cortex is located in which lobe?
questions.correctText: Frontal

questions.prompt: The somatosensory cortex is located in which lobe?
questions.correctText: Parietal

questions.prompt: The temporal lobes are primarily involved in:
questions.correctText: Auditory processing

questions.prompt: Association areas of the cortex are best described as:
choices: Regions that integrate sensory and motor information for higher mental processes
Areas dedicated solely to one sensory modality
Regions that store all autobiographical memories
Brain structures inactive during waking states
questions.correctText: Regions that integrate sensory and motor information for higher mental processes

questions.prompt: The corpus callosum is the:
choices: Cluster of nuclei in the brainstem
Bundle of axons connecting the two cerebral hemispheres
Layer of cells at the back of the eye
Folded outer surface of the cerebrum
questions.correctText: Bundle of axons connecting the two cerebral hemispheres

questions.prompt: Split-brain patients have had which structure surgically severed?
questions.correctText: Corpus callosum

questions.prompt: Hemispheric lateralization research often shows that the left hemisphere is specialized for:
questions.correctText: Language and analytical processing

questions.prompt: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to:
choices: Generate new neurons at a rapid rate in adults
Reorganize itself by forming new neural connections
Maintain a fixed structure across the lifespan
questions.correctText: Reorganize itself by forming new neural connections

questions.prompt: Which gland is considered the 'master gland' of the endocrine system?
questions.correctText: Pituitary

questions.prompt: The adrenal glands release which hormones during stress?
questions.correctText: Epinephrine and cortisol

questions.prompt: Melatonin, which helps regulate sleep, is secreted by the:
questions.correctText: Pineal gland

questions.prompt: Heritability estimates apply to:
choices: Trait variation within populations, not individuals
questions.correctText: Trait variation within populations, not individuals

questions.prompt: Adoption studies are useful because they help researchers separate:
questions.correctText: Genetic and environmental influences

questions.prompt: Compared to fraternal twins, identical twins typically show:
choices: Identical IQ scores in every case
questions.correctText: Higher correlations on heritable traits

questions.prompt: Evolutionary psychologists explain behaviors largely in terms of:
choices: How they aided survival and reproduction in ancestral environments
questions.correctText: How they aided survival and reproduction in ancestral environments

questions.prompt: Circadian rhythms refer to:
choices: Sleep cycles within a single night
Rapid eye movements within REM sleep
questions.correctText: The body's roughly 24-hour biological cycles

questions.prompt: During which sleep stage is brain activity most similar to wakefulness?
questions.correctText: REM

questions.prompt: Sleep spindles and K-complexes are characteristic of:
questions.correctText: NREM Stage 2

questions.prompt: Deep slow-wave sleep with delta waves occurs in:
questions.correctText: NREM Stage 3

questions.prompt: Sleep paralysis during REM sleep functions to:
choices: Conserve energy by lowering heart rate
Prevent the body from acting out dreams
Block all sensory input from the environment
questions.correctText: Prevent the body from acting out dreams

questions.prompt: Insomnia is characterized by:
choices: Sudden uncontrollable sleep attacks during the day
Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep
questions.correctText: Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep

questions.prompt: Narcolepsy is characterized by:
choices: Sudden attacks of overwhelming sleepiness, often into REM directly
questions.correctText: Sudden attacks of overwhelming sleepiness, often into REM directly

questions.prompt: Sleep apnea is best defined as:
choices: Repeated cessations of breathing during sleep
questions.correctText: Repeated cessations of breathing during sleep

questions.prompt: Depressant drugs such as alcohol primarily affect the body by:
choices: Slowing neural activity and bodily functions
questions.correctText: Slowing neural activity and bodily functions

questions.prompt: Stimulant drugs such as cocaine and amphetamines primarily:
choices: Produce dream-like hallucinations as their main effect
questions.correctText: Increase neural activity and arousal

questions.prompt: Hallucinogens such as LSD primarily:
choices: Distort perception and evoke sensory images without sensory input
Slow neural activity and induce sleep
questions.correctText: Distort perception and evoke sensory images without sensory input

questions.prompt: Opioids such as heroin act primarily by:
choices: Blocking dopamine at all reward sites
Mimicking the brain's endorphins to reduce pain
questions.correctText: Mimicking the brain's endorphins to reduce pain

questions.prompt: The conversion of physical energy into neural impulses is called:
questions.correctText: Transduction

questions.prompt: Absolute threshold is defined as the minimum stimulus needed to detect a stimulus:
questions.correctText: 50% of the time

questions.prompt: Signal detection theory states that detecting a stimulus depends on:
choices: The absolute threshold being identical for everyone
Both stimulus intensity and observer factors like fatigue and expectations
questions.correctText: Both stimulus intensity and observer factors like fatigue and expectations

questions.prompt: Sensory adaptation explains why:
choices: We perceive subliminal stimuli more accurately
We become more sensitive to all stimuli over time
We can't be hypnotized after repeated trials
We stop noticing a constant stimulus after prolonged exposure
questions.correctText: We stop noticing a constant stimulus after prolonged exposure

questions.prompt: Weber's law states that the just-noticeable difference is:
choices: A constant proportion of the original stimulus
A fixed amount regardless of stimulus size
Always larger for visual stimuli than auditory
questions.correctText: A constant proportion of the original stimulus

questions.prompt: The retina contains two types of photoreceptors:
questions.correctText: Rods and cones

questions.prompt: Cones are concentrated in the:
questions.correctText: Fovea

questions.prompt: Rods are specialized for:
questions.correctText: Black-and-white vision in low light

questions.prompt: The trichromatic theory of color vision proposes:
choices: That color is processed only in the lateral geniculate
Three types of cones tuned to red, green, and blue light
Two opposing color channels in the retina
That all color vision is mediated by rods
questions.correctText: Three types of cones tuned to red, green, and blue light

questions.prompt: The opponent-process theory of color vision proposes that color is processed in:
choices: A single pathway with no opponent channels
questions.correctText: Opposing pairs (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white)

questions.prompt: The blind spot in vision corresponds to:
choices: The fovea where cones are densest
The optic disc where the optic nerve exits the retina
questions.correctText: The optic disc where the optic nerve exits the retina

questions.prompt: Accommodation in vision refers to:
choices: The pupil constricting in bright light
The lens changing shape to focus on near or far objects
The retina forming a new image after eye movement
questions.correctText: The lens changing shape to focus on near or far objects

questions.prompt: Place theory best explains our perception of:
questions.correctText: High-pitched sounds

questions.prompt: Frequency theory best explains our perception of:
questions.correctText: Low-pitched sounds

questions.prompt: The cochlea is involved in:
choices: Transducing sound waves into neural impulses
questions.correctText: Transducing sound waves into neural impulses

questions.prompt: Conduction deafness usually involves damage to:
questions.correctText: The eardrum or middle-ear bones

questions.prompt: Sensorineural deafness usually involves damage to:
choices: Hair cells in the cochlea or auditory nerve
questions.correctText: Hair cells in the cochlea or auditory nerve

questions.prompt: The vestibular sense provides information about:
choices: The position of muscles and joints
questions.correctText: Body position and balance

questions.prompt: Kinesthesis is best defined as the sense of:
questions.correctText: Body part position and movement

questions.prompt: Gate-control theory of pain proposes that:
choices: Endorphins are the only mechanism of pain modulation
Pain originates entirely in the brain
Tissue damage produces an identical pain in everyone
The spinal cord contains a neural 'gate' that blocks or allows pain signals
questions.correctText: The spinal cord contains a neural 'gate' that blocks or allows pain signals

questions.prompt: Bottom-up processing primarily begins with:
questions.correctText: Sensory features detected by receptors

questions.prompt: Perceptual set refers to:
choices: A failure to perceive any motion
The arrangement of receptors in the retina
A predisposition to perceive one thing rather than another
questions.correctText: A predisposition to perceive one thing rather than another

questions.prompt: Binocular cues for depth perception include:
questions.correctText: Retinal disparity

questions.prompt: Monocular cues for depth perception include:
questions.correctText: Linear perspective

questions.prompt: The phi phenomenon is an example of:
choices: Apparent motion from rapidly flashing lights
questions.correctText: Apparent motion from rapidly flashing lights

questions.prompt: Closure, proximity, and similarity are examples of:
questions.correctText: Gestalt grouping principles

questions.prompt: Change blindness illustrates that:
choices: We always notice meaningful changes in our environment
Color afterimages are caused by retinal damage
We often fail to detect large changes in a visual scene when our attention is elsewhere
questions.correctText: We often fail to detect large changes in a visual scene when our attention is elsewhere

questions.prompt: In classical conditioning, acquisition is best described as:
choices: Refusal of the organism to learn
The initial learning of the stimulus-response association
The reappearance of a previously extinguished response
The generalization of the response to similar stimuli
questions.correctText: The initial learning of the stimulus-response association

questions.prompt: Spontaneous recovery in classical conditioning refers to:
choices: Learning a new behavior without reinforcement
The reappearance of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest
questions.correctText: The reappearance of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest

questions.prompt: Stimulus generalization occurs when:
choices: A response is triggered by stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus
An organism learns to respond only to one specific stimulus
questions.correctText: A response is triggered by stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus

questions.prompt: Stimulus discrimination is the ability to:
choices: Respond differently to similar but distinct stimuli
questions.correctText: Respond differently to similar but distinct stimuli

questions.prompt: In Watson and Rayner's Little Albert study, the unconditioned stimulus was:
questions.correctText: The loud noise

questions.prompt: Taste aversion learning, demonstrated by Garcia, shows that:
choices: Some stimulus-response pairings are biologically prepared
All stimuli can be associated equally easily
questions.correctText: Some stimulus-response pairings are biologically prepared

questions.prompt: Positive reinforcement involves:
choices: Removing something unpleasant to increase a behavior
Adding something unpleasant to decrease a behavior
Adding something pleasant to increase a behavior
Removing something pleasant to decrease a behavior
questions.correctText: Adding something pleasant to increase a behavior

questions.prompt: Negative reinforcement involves:
choices: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase a behavior
Ignoring a behavior to decrease it
Adding something unpleasant to decrease a behavior
questions.correctText: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase a behavior

questions.prompt: Positive punishment involves:
choices: Adding an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior
Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
Adding a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior
questions.correctText: Adding an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior

questions.prompt: Negative punishment (omission training) involves:
choices: Adding an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior
Adding a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior
Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
questions.correctText: Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior

questions.prompt: A fixed-interval schedule reinforces a response:
choices: After a set amount of time has passed
After a varying number of responses
After a varying amount of time
After a fixed number of responses
questions.correctText: After a set amount of time has passed

questions.prompt: A variable-interval schedule reinforces a response:
choices: After a set amount of time
After a set number of responses
After a varying number of responses
questions.correctText: After unpredictable amounts of time

questions.prompt: A fixed-ratio schedule reinforces a response:
choices: After a fixed number of responses
After a variable amount of time
After a varying number of responses
After a fixed amount of time
questions.correctText: After a fixed number of responses

questions.prompt: Shaping is best defined as:
choices: Reinforcing successive approximations toward a desired behavior
Allowing an animal to learn entirely by observation
Pairing a CS with a UCS
questions.correctText: Reinforcing successive approximations toward a desired behavior

questions.prompt: Latent learning, demonstrated by Tolman's rats, shows that:
choices: Operant conditioning cannot occur in mazes
Learning can occur without immediate reinforcement and be expressed later
questions.correctText: Learning can occur without immediate reinforcement and be expressed later

questions.prompt: A primary reinforcer is:
choices: A learned reinforcer such as money or grades
A stimulus paired with a UCS
An innately satisfying stimulus such as food or water
questions.correctText: An innately satisfying stimulus such as food or water

questions.prompt: A token economy is an application of:
questions.correctText: Operant conditioning principles

questions.prompt: The three stages of memory in the Atkinson-Shiffrin model are:
questions.correctText: Sensory, short-term, long-term

questions.prompt: Iconic memory refers to:
questions.correctText: Brief visual sensory memory

questions.prompt: Echoic memory refers to:
questions.correctText: Brief auditory sensory memory

questions.prompt: Working memory, as conceptualized by Baddeley, includes:
choices: A phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad coordinated by a central executive
questions.correctText: A phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad coordinated by a central executive

questions.prompt: Semantic memory stores:
questions.correctText: General knowledge and facts

questions.prompt: Episodic memory stores:
questions.correctText: Personal experiences and events

questions.prompt: Mnemonics such as the method of loci primarily improve:
choices: The neural speed of action potentials
questions.correctText: Encoding through organized retrieval cues

questions.prompt: The serial position effect predicts better recall for:
choices: Items presented in random order only
Only items in the middle of a list
Items at the beginning and end of a list
questions.correctText: Items at the beginning and end of a list

questions.prompt: Proactive interference occurs when:
choices: Recall is enhanced by mood matching
Older memories disrupt recall of newer ones
Newer memories disrupt recall of older ones
questions.correctText: Older memories disrupt recall of newer ones

questions.prompt: Retroactive interference occurs when:
choices: Newer learning disrupts recall of older learning
Older learning disrupts recall of newer learning
An unconditioned response disrupts a conditioned response
questions.correctText: Newer learning disrupts recall of older learning

questions.prompt: Mood-congruent memory is the tendency to:
choices: Recall experiences consistent with current mood
questions.correctText: Recall experiences consistent with current mood

questions.prompt: The forgetting curve, described by Ebbinghaus, shows that:
choices: Memory loss is rapid initially and then levels off
Forgetting is a linear, gradual decline
Memories are forgotten only after sleep
questions.correctText: Memory loss is rapid initially and then levels off

questions.prompt: Flashbulb memories are:
choices: Brief images held in iconic memory
Vivid recollections of emotionally charged events
questions.correctText: Vivid recollections of emotionally charged events

questions.prompt: An algorithm guarantees:
choices: A solution if one exists, by methodically following a procedure
questions.correctText: A solution if one exists, by methodically following a procedure

questions.prompt: The representativeness heuristic involves judging the likelihood of events by:
choices: How easily examples come to mind
How well they match a prototype
questions.correctText: How well they match a prototype

questions.prompt: The framing effect demonstrates that:
choices: How information is presented influences judgments and decisions
questions.correctText: How information is presented influences judgments and decisions

questions.prompt: A longitudinal study follows:
choices: A single participant intensively in a clinical setting
questions.correctText: The same individuals over time

questions.prompt: A cross-sectional study compares:
choices: Two cultures at one time only
Different age groups at the same point in time
questions.correctText: Different age groups at the same point in time

questions.prompt: Cohort effects refer to:
choices: Differences between age groups caused by their unique historical context
questions.correctText: Differences between age groups caused by their unique historical context

questions.prompt: Teratogens are:
choices: Agents that cause prenatal harm such as alcohol or certain viruses
questions.correctText: Agents that cause prenatal harm such as alcohol or certain viruses

questions.prompt: Fetal alcohol syndrome is caused by:
choices: Infections during the third trimester only
questions.correctText: Prenatal exposure to alcohol

questions.prompt: The order of prenatal stages is:
questions.correctText: Germinal, embryonic, fetal

questions.prompt: The rooting reflex helps newborns:
choices: Startle in response to a loud sound
Turn the head toward a touch on the cheek to find a nipple
Grasp objects placed in their palm
questions.correctText: Turn the head toward a touch on the cheek to find a nipple

questions.prompt: The Moro (startle) reflex appears when:
choices: A newborn loses support and feels falling
questions.correctText: A newborn loses support and feels falling

questions.prompt: Maturation refers to:
choices: Biological growth processes that enable orderly changes in behavior
questions.correctText: Biological growth processes that enable orderly changes in behavior

questions.prompt: Mary Ainsworth's Strange Situation primarily assesses:
questions.correctText: Attachment style

questions.prompt: In the Strange Situation, securely attached infants typically:
choices: Cling and resist soothing upon reunion
Use the caregiver as a safe base and are easily soothed upon reunion
Show no preference for the caregiver
questions.correctText: Use the caregiver as a safe base and are easily soothed upon reunion

questions.prompt: Insecure-avoidant attachment is characterized by:
choices: Strong distress and resistance to soothing
Indifference to the caregiver upon reunion
questions.correctText: Indifference to the caregiver upon reunion

questions.prompt: Insecure-resistant (anxious-ambivalent) attachment is characterized by:
questions.correctText: Clinging and difficulty being soothed

questions.prompt: Imprinting, demonstrated by Konrad Lorenz, occurs:
choices: During a critical period in some species
questions.correctText: During a critical period in some species

questions.prompt: Diana Baumrind's authoritarian parenting style is characterized by:
questions.correctText: High demands and low responsiveness

questions.prompt: Permissive parenting is characterized by:
questions.correctText: Low demands and high responsiveness

questions.prompt: Uninvolved (neglectful) parenting is characterized by:
questions.correctText: Low demands and low responsiveness

questions.prompt: Temperament refers to:
choices: Parenting style of the primary caregiver
An infant's biologically based emotional and behavioral style
questions.correctText: An infant's biologically based emotional and behavioral style

questions.prompt: In Piaget's preoperational stage, children typically show:
choices: Object permanence for the first time
questions.correctText: Egocentrism and lack of conservation

questions.prompt: Conservation, the understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in shape, develops in:
questions.correctText: The concrete operational stage

questions.prompt: Abstract reasoning and hypothetical thinking characterize Piaget's:
questions.correctText: Formal operational stage

questions.prompt: Egocentrism in Piaget's theory refers to:
questions.correctText: Difficulty taking another's perspective

questions.prompt: Assimilation in Piaget's theory means:
choices: Adjusting schemas to incorporate new information
Fitting new information into existing schemas
questions.correctText: Fitting new information into existing schemas

questions.prompt: Accommodation in Piaget's theory means:
choices: Modifying existing schemas to incorporate new information
Fitting new information into existing schemas
questions.correctText: Modifying existing schemas to incorporate new information

questions.prompt: Theory of mind refers to the understanding that:
choices: Others have beliefs, desires, and intentions that can differ from one's own
Objects continue to exist when out of sight
Quantity remains the same despite shape change
questions.correctText: Others have beliefs, desires, and intentions that can differ from one's own

questions.prompt: Vygotsky's zone of proximal development is:
choices: The range of tasks a learner can do with help but not yet alone
questions.correctText: The range of tasks a learner can do with help but not yet alone

questions.prompt: Scaffolding in Vygotsky's theory refers to:
choices: Temporary support that is gradually withdrawn as learners gain skill
questions.correctText: Temporary support that is gradually withdrawn as learners gain skill

questions.prompt: Private speech (children talking aloud to themselves) is, according to Vygotsky:
choices: A sign of egocentrism that has no function
A tool for self-guidance that eventually becomes inner speech
questions.correctText: A tool for self-guidance that eventually becomes inner speech

questions.prompt: Kohlberg's conventional level of moral reasoning emphasizes:
questions.correctText: Social approval and maintaining laws

questions.prompt: Kohlberg's postconventional level of moral reasoning involves:
choices: Abstract ethical principles that may override laws
questions.correctText: Abstract ethical principles that may override laws

questions.prompt: Carol Gilligan critiqued Kohlberg's theory by emphasizing:
choices: A care orientation in moral reasoning often seen in women
questions.correctText: A care orientation in moral reasoning often seen in women

questions.prompt: Erikson's trust vs. mistrust stage occurs during:
questions.correctText: Infancy

questions.prompt: Erikson's autonomy vs. shame and doubt stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Toddlerhood

questions.prompt: Erikson's initiative vs. guilt stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Preschool years

questions.prompt: Erikson's industry vs. inferiority stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Elementary school years

questions.prompt: Erikson's intimacy vs. isolation stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Young adulthood

questions.prompt: Erikson's generativity vs. stagnation stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Middle adulthood

questions.prompt: Erikson's integrity vs. despair stage occurs in:
questions.correctText: Late adulthood

questions.prompt: James Marcia's identity statuses include:
questions.correctText: Diffusion, foreclosure, moratorium, achievement

questions.prompt: Crystallized intelligence:
choices: Refers to problem-solving speed and novel reasoning, declining with age
Is measured only by reaction time
Reflects accumulated knowledge and tends to remain stable or grow with age
questions.correctText: Reflects accumulated knowledge and tends to remain stable or grow with age

questions.prompt: Fluid intelligence:
choices: Is measured only by vocabulary tests
Reflects accumulated knowledge stable with age
Involves problem solving in novel situations and tends to decline with age
questions.correctText: Involves problem solving in novel situations and tends to decline with age

questions.prompt: Menopause typically occurs:
choices: In midlife, marking the end of menstruation
questions.correctText: In midlife, marking the end of menstruation

questions.prompt: Erik Erikson would describe a 70-year-old reflecting positively on their life as exhibiting:
questions.correctText: Integrity

questions.prompt: Babbling typically begins around:
questions.correctText: 4 months of age

questions.prompt: A telegraphic speech sample like 'want cookie' typically appears around:
questions.correctText: 2 years of age

questions.prompt: The critical period hypothesis for language proposes that:
choices: Language is independent of brain development
Language can never be acquired after age 5
Language is most easily acquired in early childhood
Adults learn languages more easily than children
questions.correctText: Language is most easily acquired in early childhood

questions.prompt: Visual cliff experiments by Gibson and Walk showed that infants:
choices: Develop depth perception by about the age they crawl
Have no preference for any visual stimulus
Are born with full depth perception in their first hours
Never develop depth perception until age 3
questions.correctText: Develop depth perception by about the age they crawl

questions.prompt: Howard Gardner proposed:
choices: Multiple distinct intelligences (e.g., linguistic, spatial, musical)
Two types: fluid and crystallized only
questions.correctText: Multiple distinct intelligences (e.g., linguistic, spatial, musical)

questions.prompt: Robert Sternberg's triarchic theory of intelligence proposes:
questions.correctText: Analytical, creative, and practical intelligences

questions.prompt: The Flynn effect refers to:
choices: Decline in fluid intelligence with age
The rise in average IQ scores across generations
questions.correctText: The rise in average IQ scores across generations

questions.prompt: The Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (WAIS) is best known for:
choices: Providing both verbal and performance intelligence scores
questions.correctText: Providing both verbal and performance intelligence scores

questions.prompt: Stanford-Binet IQ was originally calculated as:
choices: Chronological age divided by mental age
Percentile rank among same-age peers only
Mental age divided by chronological age times 100
questions.correctText: Mental age divided by chronological age times 100

questions.prompt: A test that consistently produces similar scores for the same person across administrations has high:
questions.correctText: Reliability

questions.prompt: A test that accurately measures the construct it claims to measure has high:
questions.correctText: Validity

questions.prompt: Standardization in intelligence testing refers to:
choices: Defining meaningful scores by comparing them to a pretested group
Ensuring tests are translated into many languages
questions.correctText: Defining meaningful scores by comparing them to a pretested group

questions.prompt: Intellectual disability is typically diagnosed when an individual's IQ score is:
choices: About 70 or below, with adaptive deficits
Between 70 and 85, regardless of adaptive function
questions.correctText: About 70 or below, with adaptive deficits

questions.prompt: Carol Dweck's research on mindset distinguishes between:
choices: Internal vs. external locus of control
A fixed mindset and a growth mindset
questions.correctText: A fixed mindset and a growth mindset

questions.prompt: Adolescent egocentrism, as described by David Elkind, includes the:
questions.correctText: Imaginary audience and personal fable

questions.prompt: A securely attached infant in Ainsworth's research typically explores when the caregiver is present and:
choices: Is indifferent when the caregiver leaves
Shows distress when the caregiver leaves and seeks comfort upon return
questions.correctText: Shows distress when the caregiver leaves and seeks comfort upon return

questions.prompt: Dementia symptoms most commonly include:
choices: Progressive cognitive decline affecting memory and reasoning
questions.correctText: Progressive cognitive decline affecting memory and reasoning

questions.prompt: The self-serving bias is the tendency to:
choices: Attribute successes to external causes only
See the world as fundamentally fair
Attribute successes to internal causes and failures to external causes
questions.correctText: Attribute successes to internal causes and failures to external causes

questions.prompt: The just-world hypothesis is the belief that:
choices: People generally get what they deserve
questions.correctText: People generally get what they deserve

questions.prompt: Actor-observer bias refers to attributing:
choices: Our own behavior to situations and others' behavior to dispositions
questions.correctText: Our own behavior to situations and others' behavior to dispositions

questions.prompt: Foot-in-the-door technique works by:
choices: Offering a free sample then withdrawing it
First obtaining a small commitment, then a larger one
Asking for a very large request first
questions.correctText: First obtaining a small commitment, then a larger one

questions.prompt: Door-in-the-face technique works by:
choices: Asking for a large request first, then a smaller real request
questions.correctText: Asking for a large request first, then a smaller real request

questions.prompt: Festinger's cognitive dissonance theory predicts that people are motivated to:
choices: Reduce inconsistency between attitudes and behavior
questions.correctText: Reduce inconsistency between attitudes and behavior

questions.prompt: The mere exposure effect is the tendency to:
choices: Like stimuli more after repeated exposure
questions.correctText: Like stimuli more after repeated exposure

questions.prompt: The central route to persuasion involves:
choices: Focusing on superficial cues like attractiveness
Focusing on logic, evidence, and content of arguments
questions.correctText: Focusing on logic, evidence, and content of arguments

questions.prompt: The peripheral route to persuasion relies on:
choices: Superficial cues such as attractiveness or status of source
questions.correctText: Superficial cues such as attractiveness or status of source

questions.prompt: Normative social influence leads to conformity because people:
choices: Want to be liked and accepted by a group
questions.correctText: Want to be liked and accepted by a group

questions.prompt: Informational social influence leads to conformity because people:
choices: Believe the group has accurate information
Want to be liked by the group
questions.correctText: Believe the group has accurate information

questions.prompt: In Solomon Asch's line-judgment study, conformity decreased significantly when:
choices: Group size grew to twelve confederates
A single confederate dissented from the group
Both single dissent and private responses, but particularly dissent
questions.correctText: A single confederate dissented from the group

questions.prompt: Milgram's obedience study indicated that obedience was lowered when:
choices: The experimenter was not physically present
questions.correctText: The experimenter was not physically present

questions.prompt: Social facilitation predicts that the presence of others:
choices: Improves performance on easy or well-learned tasks
questions.correctText: Improves performance on easy or well-learned tasks

questions.prompt: Social loafing is the tendency for people to:
choices: Exert less effort in a group than alone
Work harder in groups than alone
questions.correctText: Exert less effort in a group than alone

questions.prompt: Deindividuation describes loss of self-awareness and self-restraint that can occur:
choices: In group situations that foster anonymity and arousal
questions.correctText: In group situations that foster anonymity and arousal

questions.prompt: Irving Janis coined which term to describe faulty group decision-making by cohesive groups?
questions.correctText: Groupthink

questions.prompt: Latané and Darley's research on the bystander effect identified two key processes:
choices: Noticing the event and assuming responsibility
questions.correctText: Noticing the event and assuming responsibility

questions.prompt: Out-group homogeneity bias is the tendency to:
choices: See out-group members as more similar to one another than they are
Conform only to one's own group
Treat in-group members all the same
questions.correctText: See out-group members as more similar to one another than they are

questions.prompt: Stereotype threat, identified by Claude Steele, can lead to:
choices: Underperformance when individuals fear confirming a negative stereotype
questions.correctText: Underperformance when individuals fear confirming a negative stereotype

questions.prompt: Realistic conflict theory proposes that prejudice grows from:
questions.correctText: Competition over scarce resources

questions.prompt: The frustration-aggression hypothesis states that:
choices: Frustration creates anger that can lead to aggression
Frustration only causes withdrawal, not aggression
Aggression is learned only through punishment
questions.correctText: Frustration creates anger that can lead to aggression

questions.prompt: Mere proximity tends to increase attraction because of:
questions.correctText: The mere exposure effect

questions.prompt: Robert Sternberg's triangular theory of love consists of:
questions.correctText: Intimacy, passion, and commitment

questions.prompt: Reciprocity in attraction refers to:
questions.correctText: Liking those who like us

questions.prompt: Prosocial behavior is best defined as:
choices: Behavior intended to benefit another person
questions.correctText: Behavior intended to benefit another person

questions.prompt: Freud's structure of the mind includes:
questions.correctText: Id, ego, and superego

questions.prompt: Freud believed the id operates on the:
questions.correctText: Pleasure principle

questions.prompt: Freud's ego operates on the:
questions.correctText: Reality principle

questions.prompt: Freud's superego operates on the:
questions.correctText: Moral principle

questions.prompt: The Freudian defense mechanism of projection involves:
choices: Returning to an earlier developmental stage
Attributing one's own unacceptable impulses to others
Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activities
questions.correctText: Attributing one's own unacceptable impulses to others

questions.prompt: The Freudian defense mechanism of displacement involves:
choices: Redirecting impulses toward a safer target
questions.correctText: Redirecting impulses toward a safer target

questions.prompt: Sublimation, as a defense mechanism, involves:
choices: Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activities
questions.correctText: Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activities

questions.prompt: Reaction formation involves:
choices: Expressing the opposite of one's true feelings
questions.correctText: Expressing the opposite of one's true feelings

questions.prompt: Regression as a defense mechanism involves:
choices: Reverting to behavior characteristic of an earlier stage
questions.correctText: Reverting to behavior characteristic of an earlier stage

questions.prompt: Rationalization as a defense mechanism involves:
choices: Creating justifications to make unacceptable behavior appear logical
questions.correctText: Creating justifications to make unacceptable behavior appear logical

questions.prompt: Carl Jung's concept of the collective unconscious includes:
questions.correctText: Archetypes shared across humanity

questions.prompt: Alfred Adler emphasized which motivating force in personality?
choices: The drive to overcome feelings of inferiority
questions.correctText: The drive to overcome feelings of inferiority

questions.prompt: Karen Horney challenged Freud's theory by emphasizing:
choices: Cultural and social factors and basic anxiety
questions.correctText: Cultural and social factors and basic anxiety

questions.prompt: Gordon Allport classified traits as:
questions.correctText: Cardinal, central, and secondary

questions.prompt: The Big Five trait dimensions are commonly remembered with the acronym:
questions.correctText: OCEAN

questions.prompt: Hans Eysenck originally proposed which two major personality dimensions?
questions.correctText: Extraversion-introversion and emotional stability-neuroticism

questions.prompt: The 'O' in the Big Five OCEAN refers to:
questions.correctText: Openness to experience

questions.prompt: The 'C' in OCEAN refers to:
questions.correctText: Conscientiousness

questions.prompt: The 'N' in OCEAN refers to:
questions.correctText: Neuroticism

questions.prompt: Maslow's hierarchy of needs places physiological needs:
questions.correctText: At the bottom (most basic)

questions.prompt: Rogers's concept of unconditional positive regard means:
questions.correctText: Acceptance regardless of behavior

questions.prompt: Rogers's idea of the self-concept includes:
choices: The ideal self and the real self
questions.correctText: The ideal self and the real self

questions.prompt: Albert Bandura's concept of reciprocal determinism states that behavior, environment, and personal factors:
questions.correctText: Mutually influence each other

questions.prompt: Julian Rotter's internal locus of control is associated with the belief that:
questions.correctText: One's own effort determines outcomes

questions.prompt: The MMPI is best classified as a:
choices: Behavioral checklist filled out by parents
questions.correctText: Self-report objective personality inventory

questions.prompt: The Rorschach Inkblot Test is best classified as a:
questions.correctText: Projective test

questions.prompt: The Thematic Apperception Test (TAT) asks examinees to:
questions.correctText: Tell stories about ambiguous pictures

questions.prompt: Yerkes-Dodson law states that performance peaks at:
questions.correctText: Moderate levels of arousal

questions.prompt: Intrinsic motivation refers to:
choices: Engaging in an activity for its own sake
Engaging in an activity for external rewards
questions.correctText: Engaging in an activity for its own sake

questions.prompt: Extrinsic motivation refers to:
choices: Engaging in an activity for external rewards or to avoid punishment
Engaging in an activity for its own sake
questions.correctText: Engaging in an activity for external rewards or to avoid punishment

questions.prompt: The Cannon-Bard theory of emotion proposes that:
choices: Cognitive labeling is the sole cause of emotion
Physiological response and emotional experience occur simultaneously
questions.correctText: Physiological response and emotional experience occur simultaneously

questions.prompt: Lazarus's cognitive-mediational theory of emotion emphasizes:
choices: Cognitive appraisal of a situation precedes emotion
questions.correctText: Cognitive appraisal of a situation precedes emotion

questions.prompt: Paul Ekman's research on facial expressions suggests that:
choices: Basic emotional expressions are recognized across cultures
Emotion does not appear on the face
questions.correctText: Basic emotional expressions are recognized across cultures

questions.prompt: Lymphocyte count tends to decrease under chronic stress, suggesting that:
questions.correctText: Stress can suppress immune function

questions.prompt: The biopsychosocial model of disorders explains psychopathology as:
choices: A product of biological, psychological, and sociocultural influences
questions.correctText: A product of biological, psychological, and sociocultural influences

questions.prompt: The medical model views psychological disorders as:
choices: Illnesses with biological causes that can be diagnosed and treated
Purely social labels with no biological basis
questions.correctText: Illnesses with biological causes that can be diagnosed and treated

questions.prompt: Comorbidity refers to:
choices: The co-occurrence of two or more disorders in the same individual
questions.correctText: The co-occurrence of two or more disorders in the same individual

questions.prompt: Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterized by:
choices: Fear of a specific object or situation
Persistent, uncontrollable worry without a specific trigger
questions.correctText: Persistent, uncontrollable worry without a specific trigger

questions.prompt: A panic disorder diagnosis requires:
choices: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry about future attacks
Persistent fear of one specific situation
questions.correctText: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry about future attacks

questions.prompt: A specific phobia is best defined as:
choices: An intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation
questions.correctText: An intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation

questions.prompt: Agoraphobia is the fear of:
choices: Being in places where escape might be difficult
questions.correctText: Being in places where escape might be difficult

questions.prompt: Social anxiety disorder is characterized by:
choices: Intense fear of being judged or embarrassed in social situations
questions.correctText: Intense fear of being judged or embarrassed in social situations

questions.prompt: PTSD (post-traumatic stress disorder) is now classified in the DSM-5 under:
questions.correctText: Trauma- and stressor-related disorders

questions.prompt: OCD in the DSM-5 is classified under:
questions.correctText: Obsessive-compulsive and related disorders

questions.prompt: Major depressive disorder requires symptoms persisting for at least:
questions.correctText: Two weeks

questions.prompt: Bipolar I disorder is distinguished from bipolar II by:
choices: At least one full manic episode
questions.correctText: At least one full manic episode

questions.prompt: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) is best described as:
choices: A brief episode of extreme sadness
A chronic, low-grade depressed mood lasting two years or more
questions.correctText: A chronic, low-grade depressed mood lasting two years or more

questions.prompt: Seasonal affective disorder is most often treated with:
questions.correctText: Light therapy

questions.prompt: Negative symptoms of schizophrenia include:
questions.correctText: Flat affect and avolition

questions.prompt: Delusions of grandeur involve:
choices: Loss of contact with reality is absent
False beliefs of inflated importance, power, or identity
False beliefs that others are plotting harm
questions.correctText: False beliefs of inflated importance, power, or identity

questions.prompt: Delusions of persecution involve:
choices: False beliefs that others intend to harm one
Beliefs that thoughts are being broadcast
questions.correctText: False beliefs that others intend to harm one

questions.prompt: Schizophrenia diagnosis requires symptoms for at least:
questions.correctText: Six months

questions.prompt: The dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia proposes:
choices: GABA imbalance is the sole cause
Excess dopamine activity contributes to positive symptoms
questions.correctText: Excess dopamine activity contributes to positive symptoms

questions.prompt: Dissociative identity disorder (DID) is characterized by:
choices: The presence of two or more distinct identity states
questions.correctText: The presence of two or more distinct identity states

questions.prompt: Anorexia nervosa is characterized primarily by:
choices: Episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors
Restriction of food intake and intense fear of gaining weight
questions.correctText: Restriction of food intake and intense fear of gaining weight

questions.prompt: Bulimia nervosa is characterized by:
choices: Severe weight restriction without binge eating
Persistent low mood and loss of energy
Binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as purging
questions.correctText: Binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as purging

questions.prompt: Antisocial personality disorder is characterized by:
choices: A pattern of disregard for the rights of others, often without remorse
Intense fear of abandonment and unstable relationships
questions.correctText: A pattern of disregard for the rights of others, often without remorse

questions.prompt: Borderline personality disorder is characterized by:
choices: Instability in relationships, self-image, and emotions
Persistent low mood for two years
questions.correctText: Instability in relationships, self-image, and emotions

questions.prompt: Narcissistic personality disorder is characterized by:
choices: Grandiosity, need for admiration, and lack of empathy
questions.correctText: Grandiosity, need for admiration, and lack of empathy

questions.prompt: Autism spectrum disorder is characterized by:
choices: Persistent deficits in social communication and restricted, repetitive behaviors
questions.correctText: Persistent deficits in social communication and restricted, repetitive behaviors

questions.prompt: ADHD is characterized by:
questions.correctText: Persistent inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity

questions.prompt: Free association, a technique used by Freud, requires the client to:
choices: Say whatever comes to mind without censorship
Memorize a list of trigger words
questions.correctText: Say whatever comes to mind without censorship

questions.prompt: Transference in psychoanalysis refers to:
choices: Redirecting feelings toward the therapist that originated with another person
questions.correctText: Redirecting feelings toward the therapist that originated with another person

questions.prompt: Carl Rogers's client-centered therapy emphasizes:
choices: Empathy, genuineness, and unconditional positive regard
questions.correctText: Empathy, genuineness, and unconditional positive regard

questions.prompt: Active listening, a Rogerian technique, involves:
choices: Reflecting and paraphrasing the client's statements
questions.correctText: Reflecting and paraphrasing the client's statements

questions.prompt: Exposure therapies treat anxiety disorders by:
choices: Confronting feared stimuli to reduce conditioned fear
questions.correctText: Confronting feared stimuli to reduce conditioned fear

questions.prompt: In aversive conditioning, an unwanted behavior is paired with:
choices: A pleasant stimulus to reinforce it
An unpleasant stimulus to reduce the behavior
A neutral stimulus to extinguish it
questions.correctText: An unpleasant stimulus to reduce the behavior

questions.prompt: Aaron Beck's cognitive triad describes negative views of:
choices: The self, the world, and the future
questions.correctText: The self, the world, and the future

questions.prompt: Albert Ellis's rational emotive behavior therapy (REBT) emphasizes:
choices: Disputing irrational beliefs that cause emotional distress
questions.correctText: Disputing irrational beliefs that cause emotional distress

questions.prompt: Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) typically combines:
questions.correctText: Cognitive restructuring and behavioral techniques

questions.prompt: Group therapy offers a unique benefit by:
choices: Eliminating the need for trained therapists
Allowing clients to receive support and feedback from peers with similar issues
Always being more effective than individual therapy
questions.correctText: Allowing clients to receive support and feedback from peers with similar issues

questions.prompt: Family therapy primarily treats the family as:
choices: A system of interrelated members rather than individuals in isolation
questions.correctText: A system of interrelated members rather than individuals in isolation

questions.prompt: Lithium is most commonly used to treat:
questions.correctText: Bipolar disorder

questions.prompt: Atypical antipsychotics differ from typical antipsychotics in that they:
choices: Treat depression more effectively than antipsychosis
Are entirely free of side effects
Affect serotonin as well as dopamine and have different side-effect profiles
questions.correctText: Affect serotonin as well as dopamine and have different side-effect profiles

questions.prompt: ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) is most often used to treat:
choices: Severe depression that has not responded to other treatments
questions.correctText: Severe depression that has not responded to other treatments

questions.prompt: Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) involves:
choices: Applying magnetic pulses to stimulate or inhibit cortical activity
questions.correctText: Applying magnetic pulses to stimulate or inhibit cortical activity

questions.prompt: Psychosurgery, such as the historical lobotomy, was used to:
choices: Treat ADHD by stimulating the cerebellum
Sever connections in the frontal lobes to alter behavior
questions.correctText: Sever connections in the frontal lobes to alter behavior

questions.prompt: Benzodiazepines are most commonly prescribed for:
questions.correctText: Anxiety and acute panic

questions.prompt: Hans Eysenck's early challenge to psychotherapy claimed that:
choices: Improvement rates in therapy were no better than spontaneous remission
Behavior therapy was the only effective approach
Therapy was clearly more effective than no treatment
questions.correctText: Improvement rates in therapy were no better than spontaneous remission
